sub_parts.a:
Time complexity of a program or algorithm is the growth or rate of that algorithm or program as the size of input increases.

Asymptotic notations are different ways of representing the time complexity of algorithms.

These notations depend on different assumptions of the program input and the primitive operations such as comparisons, assignments, arithmetic modifications and so on.

Primitive operations are low-level operations that have an execution time of 1, given that is, constant execution time.

The three types of Asymptotic notations are:

i) Worst-case Time complexity: Big-O Notation - [O]
- This notation represents the upper bound, that is, the maximum time that the program will take to execute.
- This is considered as the worst-case time complexity.

ii) Omega Notation [Ω]:
- This notation represents the lower bound, that is the minimum time of execution of the program for given input size 'n'.
- This is the best-case time complexity.

iii) Theta Notation [Θ]:
- This notation represents the exact bound, that is the approximate exact time of execution of the program.
- This is considered as the average case time complexity.

Graph of order of growth:
[Diagram showing a graph with axes 'size of input (n) --> increase' and 'Asymptotic Notation'. Three curves are labeled: Big-O (top), Theta (middle), Omega (bottom).]

This graph shows that for any given input size 'n':
- The time complexity of Big-O Notation will be maximum.

- The time complexity of Omega Notation will be least.

- The time complexity of Theta Notation will be close to the actual execution time and will be between Big-O and Omega.

ADT or Abstract Data Type are types of data types that provide only the essential information and hide the other details.

ADTs are a type of object whose logical behaviour is defined by a set of values and operations that are hidden.'

ADT focuses on the what to do rather than how to do it and are called abstract because they provide an implementation-independent view.

The implementation of ADTs require special data structures to store the data and algorithms to perform basic operations.

Data Structures are an implementation of Abstract Data Type.

They provide only the how to do by not defining how the data is stored in memory.

Data Structures also use certain algorithms that are hidden from view, to perform operations.

Example of such a data structure is stack which stores elements in last in, first out manner.

Stack performs ADT operations like push(), pop(), top(), isEmpty() for insertion, deletion, getting top-most value etc respectively.

sub_parts.a:
ADT or Abstract Data Type is a data type that only gives essential data information and hides implementation details.

Arrays are also an implementation of Abstract Data Type since they

Array as ADT is the following:

i) Arrays store elements sequentially in contiguous memory locations, that can be accessed by traversing through the array.

ii) Arrays have fixed spaces since arrays are static data types, they allocate memory at compile-time and this size cannot be altered.

iii) Arrays also provide Random Access to elements.

The operations performed on arrays are:

i) Insertion:

Insertion in an array is done by traversing through the array to get to desired position and inserting the element

- This operation has a best-case time complexity of 1, that is, if element is to be inserted at the start of the array. O(1)

- The worst-case time complexity is n when the element has to be inserted at the end. O(n),
when where n = number of elements in array.

ii) Search:

Searching in an array can be done by traversing through the arrays using the following:

a) Linear Search - sequentially searching all elements to find the target. Time

- Time Complexity: O(n), when element is at last position.

b) Binary Search - we divide finding the middle element by dividing the elements into two halves continuously till target is found.

- Time Complexity: O(log n)

iii) Deletion:

- It requires traversing the array to find element to be deleted and deleting it if found.
- Time Complexity: O(n)

iv) Sorting:

- Used to sort the elements of the array in ascending or descending order.
- Uses multiple algorithms or techniques for sorting:

a) Selection sort → Time complexity: O(n²)

b) Bubble sort → Time complexity: O(n²)

c) Quick sort → Time complexity: O(nlog n)
sub_parts.b:
Bubble sort is a sorting technique or algorithm that sorts elements in ascending or descending order depending on the requirement.

Bubble sort performs compare adjacent elements and swaps them if they are not in the correct order.

This sorting algorithm takes (n-1) passes to complete where n = number of elements to be sorted.

After each pass, one element is sorted, for example, if sorting in ascending order is required, then after the first pass, the largest element will be sorted to the end of the sequence.

Time Complexity of Bubble Sort is O(n²) in worst-case scenario, but this can be optimized to O(n) for best-case scenario.

Best-case scenario is when the elements are already sorted.

list = [7, 4, 10, 6, 3, 12, 1, 8, 2, 15]

Step 1:
- Compare 7 & 4.
Since 7 > 4 thus, swap them.
=> 4, 7

- Comparing 7 and 10 => no change.
- Comparing 10 and 6 => 10 > 6, thus swap
- Swap           => 4, 7, 6, 10

- Comparing 10 and 3 => 10 > 3, thus swap.
=> 4, 7, 6, 3, 10

- Comparing 10 and 12 => no change
=> 4, 7, 6, 3, 10, 12

- Comparing 12 and 1 => 12 > 1, thus swap
=> 4, 7, 6, 3, 10, 1, 12

- Comparing 12 and 8 => 12 > 8, swap.
=> 4, 7, 6, 3, 10, 1, 8, 12

- Comparing 12 and 2 => no change. 12 > 2, swap.
=> 4, 7, 6, 3, 10, 8, 2, 12

- Comparing 12 and 15 => no change.
=> 4, 7, 6, 3, 10, 8, 2, 12, 15

Thus, after 1st pass the array is:
4, 7, 6, 3, 10, 8, 2, 12, 15

In this, the largest element, 15, is placed in last position.

Step 2:
- Compare 4 and 7 => no change

- Compare 7 and 6
=> 4, 6, 7, 3, 10, 8, 2, 12, 15

- Compare 7 and 3 => no change swap
=> 4, 6, 3, 7, 10, 8, 2, 12, 15

- Compare 7 and 10 => no change

- Compare 10 and 8 => swap
=> 4, 6, 3, 7, 8, 10, 2, 12, 15

- Compare 10 and 2 => swap
=> 4, 6, 3, 7, 8, 2, 10, 12, 15

- Compare 10 and 12 => no change.

Thus, after 2nd pass, the second largest element is in right place.

4, 6, 3, 7, 8, 2, 10, 12, 15

4, 6, 3, 7, 8, 2, 10, 12, 15
Step 3:

- Compare 4 and 6 => no change

- 3 and 6 => swap
=> 4, 3, 6, 7, 8, 2, 10, 12, 15

- 6 and 7 => no change

- 7 and 8 => no change

- 8 and 2 => swap
=> 4, 3, 6, 7, 2, 8, 10, 12, 15

- 8 and 10 => no change.
after 3rd pass, 3rd largest element is sorted.
Step 4:

- 4 and 3 => swap
=> 3, 4, 6, 7, 2, 8, 10, 12, 15

- 4 and 6 => no change

- 6 and 7 => no change

- 7 and 2 => swap
=> 3, 4, 6, 2, 7, 8, 10, 12, 15

After 4th pass, 4th largest is in place.
Step 5:

- 3 & 4 => no change

- 4 & 6 => no change

- 6 & 2 => swap
=> 3, 4, 2, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12, 15

After 5th pass, 5th largest, 7, is in place.
Step 6:

- 3 & 4 => no change

- 4 & 2 => swap
=> 3, 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12, 15

Remaining => no change
After 6th pass, 6 is in place.
Step 7:

- 3 & 2 => swap
=> 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12, 15

Remaining no change.

- The next two steps will not cause any changes and the array is sorted in 7 passes, but total 9 passes will happen.

sub_parts.a:
i) SLL or Simply Linked List is a data structure that is linear and stores data in sequential order.

- SLL has nodes and every node has at maximum one successor. All nodes are connected to each other in memory.

- SLL may or may not have nodes stored in contiguous memory locations but still, they can be accessed since each node has the memory address pointer to its next node.

- Example:
Element: (2) (4) (6) (8) -> X
memory: 101 5002 31 400
location.

- The structure of nodes in SLL is:
[Diagram: A box split into two, left part labeled 'value of element', right part labeled 'pointer which points to the memory location of next node'.]

- This is what the example looks like in memory:
[Diagram: Linked list with nodes [2|5002] -> [4|31] -> [6|400] -> [8|NULL]. 'head' pointing to first node, 'tail' pointing to last node.]

ii) DLL or Doubly Linked List is a data structure that is similar to SLL.

- In DLL, every node is connected to its next node as well as to its previous node.

- In DLL also, a node has a maximum of one successor and one predecessor and the nodes may or may not be stored in contiguous memory locations.

- Example: In DLL structure, every node stores its value, the pointer to the memory address of next node as well as previous node.

- Example: Structure of DLL:
[Diagram: A box split into three, left part labeled 'pointer to previous node', middle part labeled 'value of element', right part labeled 'pointer to next node'.]

So, DLL stores two pointers unlike SLL which only stores one pointer.

- Example:
[Diagram: Doubly linked list with nodes NULL <- [1|2|2] <-> [2|4|4] <-> [3|6|6] <-> [4|8|8] -> NULL. Pointers shown connecting elements.]

iii) CLL or Circular Linked List is a type of linked list data structure that stores in which the first node is connected to the last node and last node is connected to first node.

- It is more efficient since jumping from first node to last and vice-versa is a constant time operation (O(1)).

- It is of two types:

a) SCLL or Singularity Circular Linked list which is same as SLL except that first and last nodes are connected. The front of last node points to the first node.

b) DCLL or Doubly Circular Linked List which is same as DLL except that first and last nodes are connected. The front of last node points to first node and the front of back of first node points to last node.

- Examples:

a) SCLL
[Diagram: Circular singly linked list with nodes [2] -> [4] -> [6] -> [8]. The last node [8] points back to [2].]

b) DCLL:
[Diagram: Circular doubly linked list with nodes [2] <-> [4] <-> [6] <-> [8]. The first node [2] points back to [8] and the last node [8] points to [2].]

b) DCLL
[Diagram: Circular doubly linked list with nodes [2] <-> [4] <-> [6]. The first node [2] points back to [6] and the last node [6] points to [2].]
sub_parts.b:
Stack is an Abstract Data Type since it only gives the essential information and hides the implementation details.

Stack as an ADT is used to allows for:

i) Storage of elements in a sequential manner.

ii) LIFO access - which means Last In, First Out.
Due to this the element that goes in last is the first to be deleted.

iii) Stack operations like push() for inserting to the top of stack, pop() for deleting from the top of the stack, top() for finding the value of the element at the top (also called peek()) and so on.

Stack representation as ADT:
[Diagram: A stack with elements d, c, b, a (from top to bottom). Text next to it: 'd is inserted last and removed first (LIFO).']

It is like a stack of coins or books in which elements can only be accessed from the top.

Infix Expression: (A+B)*D+E/(F*A*D)+C

[Table for Infix to Postfix conversion]
Scanned Character | Stack | Output Answer
------------------|-------|--------------
(                 | (     | 
A                 | (     | A
+                 | (+    | A
B                 | (+    | AB
)                 | +     | AB+
*                 | *     | AB+
D                 | *     | AB+D
+                 | +     | AB+D*
E                 | +     | AB+D*E
/                 | +/    | AB+D*E
(                 | +/(   | AB+D*E
F                 | +/(   | AB+D*EF
*                 | +/(*  | AB+D*EF
A                 | +/(*  | AB+D*EFA
D                 | +/(*  | AB+D*EFAD
)                 | +/    | AB+D*EFAD*
+                 | +     | AB+D*EFAD*+
C                 | +     | AB+D*EFAD*+C
                  |       | AB+D*EFAD*+/C
                  |       | (Answer)

sub_parts.a:
Hashing is a technique which enables us to convert a large amount of data into a fixed or small value.

It is a searching technique which allows both constant to be lookup, that is, O(1), time complexity.

Hashing uses a hash function to input keys and perform some mathematical operation on them and give hash values which are the indices of keys into a hash table.

The hash table stores elements data in the form of key and values and is used in database indexing, caching and implementing data Structures like Umap, set and so on.

Hashing techniques are methods to obtain a hash value by performing mathematical operations on the keys and some of them are:

i) Division method:

- In this method the hash value is generated by taking the modulus (remainder or Division) of the input key with the size of the table.

- H(k) = k % M

Here, H(k) is the hash function which gives a hash value.
k is the input key.
M is the size of the hash table.

- It is a standard hashing technique, but may to lead to collision when more than one elements with the same modulo value are inserted into the same location.

ii) Folding method:

- This has two types:

a) Folding shift method:

- The input key is divided into many parts and each part has the same number of digits.

- The last part may have lesser digits than the other parts.

- The individual parts are then added to generate a hash key and if the sum has a carry generated, then that is discarded in the hash value.

- The technique essentially 'folds' the input key to generate the hash value.

Example:
k = 12345

- After folding, k = 12, 34, 5

- Adding the individual parts.
S = 12 + 34 + 5
= 51

H(k) = 51

k = k₁, k₂ ....... kₙ
s = k₁ + k₂ + ... + kₙ
H(k) = S

b) Folding boundary:

- This is same as folding shift but the 1st & last parts are reversed.

- Example:
k = 12345 = 12, 34, 5
=> k = 21, 34, 5

=> S = 60

=> H(k) = 60

iii) Multiplication method.

- H(k) = floor [M * (KA mod 1)]

[Diagram: 'M' is labeled as 'size of hash table']

k = input key
A = a pre-defined constant (default A = 0.6180)
floor () function gives the largest integer less than or equal to the product.

- Example:
K=107, M=50, A=0.6180 (default)

=> H(107) = floor [50 * (107 x 0.6180) % 1]

= floor [50 * (66.12 % 1)]

= floor [50 * 0.12]

= floor [6]

= 6
sub_parts.b:
Input = [4371, 1323, 6173, 4199, 4344, 9679, 1989]

M=10 (size of table)

H(k) = k % 10

i) Linear Probing:

- H(4371) = 4371 % 10
= 1

[Hash table state: index 1 has 4371]

- H(1323) = 1323 % 10
= 3

[Hash table state: index 3 has 1323]

- H(6173) = 6173 % 10
= 3 (collision)

[Hash table state: index 0 empty, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323 (with an arrow to collision), 4-9 empty]

=> Linear Probing searches for the next empty slot from index = 4 onwards and places 6173 in Index = 4

=> H(6173) = 4

[Hash table state: index 0 empty, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173, 5-9 empty]

- H(4199) = 4199 % 10
= 9

[Hash table state: index 0 empty, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173, 5 empty, 6 empty, 7 empty, 8 empty, 9 has 4199]

- H(4344) = 4344 % 10
= 4 (collision)

[Hash table state (shown incorrectly, it indicates collision at 4 and then subsequent probe): index 0 empty, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173,4344 -> collision, 5-9 empty]

Next empty slot = 5

- H(9679) = 9679 % 10
= 9 (collision)

=> Next empty slot = 0

=> H(9679) = 0

[Hash table state: index 0 has 9679, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173, 5 has 4344, 6 empty, 7 empty, 8 empty, 9 has 4199 -> collision]

- H(1989) = 1989 % 10
= 9

=> Next empty slot = 2

[Hash table state: index 0 has 9679, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173, 5 has 4344, 6 empty, 7 empty, 8 empty, 9 has 4199, 1989 -> collision]

∴ Final Hash Table:
[Table with indices 0-9]
0 | 9679
1 | 4371
2 | 1989
3 | 1323
4 | 6173
5 | 4344
6 | 
7 | 
8 | 
9 | 4199

ii) And Quadratic Probing:
H'(k) = (H(k) + i²) % M
H(k) = k % M
i = 1 to M-1 (only for collision)

- H(4371) = 4371 % 10
= 1

- H(1323) = 1323 % 10
= 3

- H(6173) = 6173 % 10
= 3 (collision)

[Hash table state: index 0 empty, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323 (and 6173, arrow to collision, then 'next empty slot'), 4-9 empty]

=> H'(6173) = (H(6173) + 1²) % 10
= (3 + 1) % 10
= 4

[Hash table state: index 0 empty, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173, 5-9 empty]

[Hash table state (partially visible, seems to be a continuation of the state for collisions): index 0 empty, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173, 4344 -> collision, 5 'next empty slot', 6-8 empty, 9 has 4199]

- H(4199) = 4199 % 10
= 9

- H(4344) = 4344 % 10
= 4 (collision) => H'(4344) = (H(4344) + 1²) % 10
= (4 + 1) % 10 = 5

[Hash table state: index 0 empty, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173, 5 has 4344, 6-8 empty, 9 has 4199, 9679 -> collision]

- H(9679) = 9679 % 10 = 9 (collision) => H'(9679, 1) = [H(9679) + 1²] % 10

=> H'(9679, 2) = [H(9679) + 4] % 10.

H'(9679, 1) = [H(9679) + 1²] % 10
= 0

[Hash table state: index 0 has 9679, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323, 4 has 6173, 5 has 4344, 6-8 empty, 9 has 4199]

- H'(1989, 2) = [H(1989) + 4] % 10

= (9 + 4) % 10
= 3 (collision)

H'(1989, 3) = (9 + 9) % 10
= 8

[Hash table state: index 0 has 9679, 1 has 4371, 2 empty, 3 has 1323 (crossed out), 4 has 6173, 5 has 4344, 6 empty, 7 empty, 8 has 1989, 9 has 4199]

sub_parts.b:
Kruskal's algorithm is a technique to find the Minimum Spanning Tree.

Algorithm:

i. Sort all the edges in ascending order.

ii. Create a disjoint set for each vertex.

iii. For an edge (u, v) in sorted edge list:
- If u and v are in different sets, add
- the edge (u, v) to MST.
- Find union of (u, v)

iv. Find the next minimum weight edge which does not form a cycle.

v. Repeat iii & iv until MST has V-1 edges.

MST:
[Table of edges and weights]
Edge | Weight
-----|------
1-2  | 10
3-4  | 12
2-7  | 14
2-3  | 16
4-7  | 18
5-7  | 22
5-6  | 24
1-2  | 28

[Multiple diagrams illustrating Kruskal's algorithm on a graph with nodes 1-7 and weighted edges, showing edges selected and 'MST' values changing from 103 to 99.]

Final Answer
